item.title: The U.S. is losing Europe faster than Americans realize
item.description: Europeans are hedging against coercion in security, trade, education — and everyday life.

item.title: It takes 29 years to open a mine. Why?
item.description: Broken permitting regulations cost the nation untold jobs and trillions of dollars.

item.title: A casket cartel tries to bury the competition
item.description: An Oklahoma couple has run afoul of a state law protecting funeral-home operators.

item.title: Globalization hasn’t stopped. The chaos over Hormuz proves it.
item.description: A globalized economy has plenty of strengths and weaknesses. It also isn’t going anywhere.

item.title: What happens when a war is over identity, not strategy
item.description: The real conflict with Iran isn’t over nuclear facilities. It’s over who gets to be the hero.

item.title: I’m tired of proving I belong in America
item.description: Minorities don’t need to show they are like everyone else.

item.title: What a Catholic feminist dares to say
item.description:
In recent years, the idea of a “crisis of men and boys” has taken center stage in the cultural conversation. That can feel like a pendulum swing: from making space for women to examining men’s struggles, rarely holding both in view at the same time. Some see this moment as a reaction to the 2010s — an era shaped by “girlboss” ambition, #MeToo and a renewed focus on women’s advancement and autonomy. So, what gets missed when these struggles are framed as separate?
Host Megan McArdle is joined by Leah Libresco Sargeant, senior policy analyst at the Niskanen Center and author of "The Dignity of Dependence: A Feminist Manifesto." Sargeant offers a different lens — challenging the idea that independence is the ultimate goal and instead pointing toward a more interconnected understanding of men and women.

item.title: Zombie flights to nowhere
item.description: The government wastes almost $700 million a year on subsidies that were supposed to expire in 1988.

item.title: Jamie Dimon’s New York warning
item.description: JPMorgan’s CEO says “no city — or company or country — has a divine right to success.”

item.title: The real victims of the bike lane removal project
item.description: Bike lane removal, student visa changes, AI and more, in readers’ eyes.

item.title: The Potomac sewage spill. What really happened?
item.description:
An upcoming Washington Post Opinions show. Stay tuned.
00:00 - Monologue 02:20 - The Potomac sewage spill. What really happened? 13:59 - Trump threatens 'Living Hell' on Iran 30:06 - Artemis II breaks space travel record. Who does this help?

item.title: Cartoon by Mike Smith
item.description: Mike Smith cartoon on Iran war, flight prices.

item.title: The backward logic of pharmaceutical tariffs
item.description: Central planners in Washington want to raise prices to lower them.

item.title: Why Abigail Spanberger became unpopular and how she can fix it
item.description: The Virginia governor is the face of a polarizing power grab.

item.title: A new study shows simple ways to avoid dementia
item.description: Keeping mentally engaged can reduce dementia risk, especially among older adults.

item.title: Who will replace Marjorie Taylor Greene? In Georgia’s 14th, I saw clues.
item.description: Democrats’ national focus on No Kings misses what local voters want from their government.

item.title: The U.S. needs to go on AI offense
item.description: Containing China’s rise is barely half a strategy.

item.title: As Viktor Orban goes, so goes MAGA?
item.description: Hungary’s election is a test case for the limits of populism’s appeal.

item.title: The ripple effects of NASA’s Artemis mission could be bigger than you think
item.description: The moon and Mars could create a new human species.

item.title: Activist local governments should not be regulators of energy markets
item.description: The Supreme Court must strike down efforts to usurp federal authority over international emissions.

item.title: Overdose deaths are plummeting. Here’s what worked.
item.description: Efforts to disrupt the supply of fentanyl are paying off. We cannot back down.

item.title: A total eclipse
item.description: Michael Ramirez cartoon on Artemis II launch, national debt.

item.title: The ICE detention of Annie Ramos marks another low
item.description: The president is again poorly served by hardliners in his administration.

item.title: A World Trade Organization is irrelevant when no one follows its rules
item.description: A stalemate between the United States and Brazil reveals how vulnerable the WTO has become.

item.title: The joy of Artemis II shows why going back to the moon matters
item.description: Artemis II, air travel and national debt, in readers’ eyes.

item.title: Will the next William Henry Harrison please stand up?
item.description: A candidate in the mold of our ninth and short-serving president is the perfect response to Trump.

item.title: Maine’s race to the top on taxes
item.description: After opposing similar tax hikes in her state, Janet Mills is supporting a millionaires tax for Maine as she runs against Graham Platner in the Senate primary.

item.title: Can Trump end the war in Iran and save face? I answered your questions.
item.description: David’s live chat with readers started at 12 p.m. ET on Monday. Read the transcript.
